greek_head: Ἱππίας ὁ καλός τε καὶ σοφός ὡς διὰ χρόνου ἡμῖν κατῆρας εἰς τὰς Ἀθήνας
greek_tokens: ippios o kalos te kai sofos ws dia xronos egw katairw eis o aqhneus ou gar sxolh eimi swkraths o gar hlis otan tis deomai diaprassw pros tis o polis aei epi proteros egw erxomai o poliths airew presbeuths hgeomai dikasths kai aggelos ikanos eimi o logos e an
english_head: Hippias, beautiful and wise, what a long time it is since you have put in at the port of Athens! I am too busy, Socrates. For whenever Elis needs to have any business transacted with any of the states, she always comes to me first of her citizens and chooses me as envoy, thinking that I am the ablest judge and messenger of the words that are spoken by the several states.

greek_head: παρὰ τῶν πόλεων ἑκάστων λέγωνται πολλάκις μὲν οὖν καὶ εἰς ἄλλας πόλεις ἐπρέσβευσα πλεῖστα δὲ καὶ περὶ
greek_tokens: para o polis ekastos legw pollakis men oun kai eis allos polis presbeuw pleistos de kai peri pleistos kai megas eis o lakedaimwn dio dh o su erwtaw ou qamizw eis ode o topos toioutos mentoi eimi ippios eimi o o alhqeia sofos te kai teleios anhr eimi su gar kai idios ikanos ei para o neos polus xrhma lambanw eti plew
english_head: So I have often gone as envoy to other states, but most often and concerning the most numerous and important matters to Lacedaemon. For that reason, then, since you ask me, I do not often come to this neighborhood. That’s what it is, Hippias, to be a truly wise and perfect man! For you are both in your private capacity able to earn much money from the young

greek_head: ὠφελεῖν ὧν λαμβάνεις καὶ αὖ δημοσίᾳ τὴν σαυτοῦ πόλιν ἱκανὸς εὐεργετεῖν ὥσπερ χρὴ τὸν μέλλοντα μὴ καταφρονήσεσθαι
greek_tokens: wfelew os lambanw kai au dhmosios o sautou polis ikanos euergetew wsper xrh o mellw mh katafronew alla eudokimew en o polus atar eimi ippios tis pote o aitios ostis e palaios ekeinos os onoma megas legw epi sofia pittakos te kai bias kai o amfi o milhsios qallw kai eti o usteron mexri anacagorhs ws h pas h e polus autos fainw apexw o politikos pracis tis de oiomai eimi swkraths allos ge h adunatos eimi
english_head: and to confer upon them still greater benefits than you receive, and in public affairs you are able to benefit your own state, as a man must who is to be not despised but held in high repute among the many. And yet, Hippias, what in the world is the reason why those men of old whose names are called great in respect to wisdom—Pittacus, and Bias, and the Milesian Thales with his followers and also the later ones, down to Anaxagoras, are all,

greek_head: καὶ οὐχ ἱκανοὶ ἐξικνεῖσθαι φρονήσει ἐπ' ἀμφότερα τά τε κοινὰ καὶ τὰ ἴδια
greek_tokens: kai ou ikanos ecikneomai fronhsis epi amfoteros o te koinos kai o idios ara oun pros zeus wsper o allos texnh epididwmi kai eimi para o nun dhmiourgos e palaios faulos outws kai o umeteros o o sofisths texnh epididwmi fhmi kai eimi o arxaios o peri o sofia faulos pros su panu men oun orqos legw ei ara nun egw eimi ippios o bia anabiow gelws
english_head: or most of them, found to refrain from affairs of state? What else do you suppose, Socrates, than that they were not able to compass by their wisdom both public and private matters? Then for Heaven’s sake, just as the other arts have progressed, and the ancients are of no account in comparison with the artisans of today, shall we say that your art also has progressed and those of the ancients who were concerned with wisdom are of no account in comparison with you? Yes, you are quite right. Then, Hippias, if Bias were to come to life again now,

greek_head: ἂν ὄφλοι πρὸς ὑμᾶς ὥσπερ καὶ τὸν Δαίδαλόν φασιν οἱ ἀνδριαντοποιοί νῦν εἰ γενόμενος τοιαῦτ' ἐργάζοιτο οἷα ἦν
greek_tokens: an ofliskanw pros su wsper kai o daidalos fhmi e andriantopoios nun ei gignomai toioutos ergazomai oios eimi apo os onoma exw katagelastos an eimi eimi men outos eimi swkraths outws ws su legw eqw mentoi egw o palaios te kai proteros egw proteros te kai mallon egkwmiazw h o nun eulabeomai men fqonos o zaw fobew de mhnis o teleutaw
english_head: he would be a laughing-stock in comparison with you, just as the sculptors say that Daedalus, if he were to be born now and were to create such works as those from which he got his reputation, would be ridiculous. That, Socrates, is exactly as you say. I, however, am in the habit of praising the ancients and our predecessors rather than the men of the present day, and more greatly, as a precaution against the envy of the living and through fear of the wrath of those who are dead.

greek_head: Καλῶς γε σύ ὦ Ἱππία ὀνομάζων τε καὶ διανοούμενος ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖς συμμαρτυρῆσαι δέ σοι ἔχω ὅτι ἀληθῆ λέγεις
greek_tokens: kalos ge su eimi ippios onomazw te kai dianoeomai ws egw dokew summarturew de su exw ostis alhqhs legw kai o eimi su epididwmi o texnh pros o kai o dhmosios prassw dunamai meta o idios gorgihs te gar outos o leontinos sofisths deuro afikneomai dhmosios oikoqen presbeuw ws ikanos eimi leontinos o koinos prassw kai en te o dhmos dokew ariston eipon kai idios epideicis poiew kai suneimi o neos xrhma polus ergazomai
english_head: Yours, Hippias, is a most excellent way, at any rate, of speaking about them and of thinking, it seems to me and I can bear you witness that you speak the truth, and that your art really has progressed in the direction of ability to carry on public together with private affairs. For this man Gorgias, the sophist from Leontini, came here from home in the public capacity of envoy, as being best able of all the citizens of Leontini to attend to the interests of the community, and it was the general opinion that he spoke excellently in the public assembly, and in his private capacity, by giving exhibitions and associating with the young, he earned and received a great deal of money from this city;

greek_head: καὶ ἔλαβεν ἐκ τῆσδε τῆς πόλεως εἰ δὲ βούλει ὁ ἡμέτερος ἑταῖρος Πρόδικος οὗτος πολλάκις μὲν καὶ ἄλλοτε δημοσίᾳ
greek_tokens: kai laptw ek ode o polis ei de boulomai o hmeteros etairos prodikos outos pollakis men kai allote dhmosios afikneomai atar o teleutaios enagxos afikneomai dhmosios ek kew legos su en o bouleus panu eudokimew kai idios epideicis poiew kai o neos suneimi xrhma laptw qaumasths osos o de palaios ekeinos oudeis pwpote aciow argurion misqos prassw oude epideicis poiew
english_head: or, if you like, our friend here, Prodicus, often went to other places in a public capacity, and the last time, just lately, when he came here in a public capacity from Ceos, he gained great reputation by his speaking before the Council, and in his private capacity, by giving exhibitions and associating with the young, he received a marvellous sum of money; but none of those ancients ever thought fit to exact the money as payment for his wisdom or to give exhibitions among people of various places;

greek_head: ἐν παντοδαποῖς ἀνθρώποις τῆς ἑαυτοῦ σοφίας οὕτως ἦσαν εὐήθεις καὶ ἐλελήθει αὐτοὺς ἀργύριον ὡς πολλοῦ
greek_tokens: en pantodapos anqrwpos o eautou sofia outws eimi euhqhs kai lanqanw autos argurion ws polus acios eaw outos de ekateros pleos argurion apo sofia ergazomai h allos dhmiourgos apo ostis texnh kai eti proteros outos prwtagoras oudeis gar eimi swkraths oida o kalh peri outos ei gar oida osos argurion ergazomai egw qaumazw an kai o men allos eaw afikneomai de pote eis sikelia prwtagoras
english_head: so simple-minded were they, and so unconscious of the fact that money is of the greatest value. But either of these two has earned more money from his wisdom than any artisan from his art. And even before these Protagoras did so. Why, Socrates, you know nothing of the beauties of this. For if you were to know how much money I have made, you would be amazed. I won’t mention the rest, but once, when I went to Sicily,

greek_head: αὐτόθι ἐπιδημοῦντος καὶ εὐδοκιμοῦντος καὶ πρεσβυτέρου ὄντος πολὺ νεώτερος ὢν ἐν ὀλίγῳ χρόνῳ πάνυ πλέον ἢ πεντήκοντα
greek_tokens: autoqi epidhmew kai eudokimew kai presbus eimi polus neos eimi en oligos xronos panu pleos h penthkonta kai ekaton mna ergazomai kai ek eis ge xwrion panu mikros inukou pleos h eikosi mna kai outos erxomai oikade ferw o pathr didwmi wste ekeinos kai o allos poliths qaumazw te kai ekplhssw kai sxedon tis oimh egw plew xrhma ergazomai h allos sunduo ostis boulomai o sofisths kalos ge eimi ippios legw kai megas tekmhrion
english_head: although Protagoras was staying there and had a great reputation and was the older, I, who was much younger, made in a very short time more than one hundred and fifty minas, and in one very small place, Inycus, more than twenty minas; and when I came home, I took this and gave it to my father, so that he and the other citizens were overwhelmed with amazement. And I pretty well think I have made more money than any other two sophists together. That’s a fine thing you say, Hippias, and strong testimony to your wisdom

greek_head: σοφίας τῆς τε σεαυτοῦ καὶ τῶν νῦν ἀνθρώπων πρὸς τοὺς ἀρχαίους ὅσον διαφέρουσι τῶν γὰρ προτέρων περὶ
greek_tokens: sofia o te sautou kai o nun anqrwpos pros o arxaios osos diaferw o gar proteros peri anacagorhs legw polus amaqia kata o sos logos enantion gar anacagorhs fhmi sumbainw h su katalimpanw gar autos polus xrhma katamelew kai apollumi pas outws autos anohtos sofizomai legw de kai peri allos o palaios eteros toioutos outos men oun egw dokew kalos tekmhrion apofainw peri sofia o
english_head: and that of the men of today and to their great superiority to the ancients. For the earlier sophists of the school of Anaxagoras must have been very ignorant to judge from what is said, according to your view; for they say that what happened to Anaxagoras was the opposite of what happens to you; for though much money was left him, he neglected it and lost it all so senseless was his wisdom. And they tell similar tales about others among the ancients. So this seems to me fine testimony that you adduce for the wisdom of the men of today as compared with the earlier men,

greek_head: νῦν πρὸς τοὺς προτέρους καὶ πολλοῖς συνδοκεῖ ὅτι τὸν σοφὸν αὐτὸν αὑτῷ μάλιστα δεῖ σοφὸν εἶναι τούτου δ' ὅρος ἐστὶν
greek_tokens: nun pros o proteros kai polus sundokew ostis o sofos autos eautou malista deomai sofos eimi outos de oros eimi ara os an pleistos argurion ergazomai kai outos men ikanos exw ode de egw eipon su autos pinw pleistos argurion ergazomai o polis eis os afikneomai h dhlos ostis ek lakedaimwn oiper kai pleistakis afikneomai ou ma o dios eimi swkraths pws fhmi alla elaxistos
english_head: and many people agree with me that the wise man must be wise for himself especially ; and the test of this is, who makes the most money. Well, so much for that. But tell me this: at which of the cities that you go to did you make the most money? Or are we to take it that it was at Lacedaemon, where your visits have been most frequent? No, by Zeus, it was not, Socrates. What’s that you say? But did you make least there?

greek_head: Οὐδὲν μὲν οὖν τὸ παράπαν πώποτε Τέρας λέγεις καὶ θαυμαστόν ὦ Ἱππία καί μοι
greek_tokens: oudeis men oun o parapan pwpote teras legw kai qaumastos eimi ippios kai egw eipon poteros o sofia o sos ou oios o suneimi autos kai manqanw eis areth beltiwn poiew kai polus ge eimi swkraths alla o men inukinwn uios oios te eimi ameinwn poiew o de spartiaths adunatew polus ge dew alla dhta sikeliwths men epiqumew ameinwn gignomai lakedaimonios
english_head: Why, I never made anything at all. That is a prodigious marvel that you tell, Hippias; and say now: is not your wisdom such as to make those who are in contact with it and learn it, better men in respect to virtue? Yes, much better, Socrates. But you were able to make the sons of the Inycenes better, and had no power to improve the sons of the Spartans? That is far from true. Well, then, the Siceliotes desire to become better, and the Lacedaemonians do not?

greek_head: δ' οὔ Πάντως γέ που ὦ Σώκρατες καὶ Λακεδαιμόνιοι Ἆρ' οὖν χρημάτων ἐνδείᾳ ἔφευγον τὴν σὴν
greek_tokens: de ou pantws ge pou eimi swkraths kai lakedaimonios ara oun xrhma endeia feugw o shs omilia ou dhta epei ikanos autos eimi tis deomai an eaw ostis epiqumew kai exw xrhma kai sew dunamai o megas autos wfelew ou plhrhs su argurion apopempw alla ekeinos mhs mh lakedaimonios sew beltiwn an paideuw o eautou pais h outos fhmi outws kai su sugxwrew
english_head: No certainly, Socrates, the Lacedaemonians also desire it. Then it was for lack of money that they avoided intercourse with you? Not at all, since they have plenty of money. What, then, could be the reason, that when they desired it and had money, and you had power to confer upon them the greatest benefits, they did not send you away loaded with money? But I see; perhaps the Lacedaemonians might educate their own children better than you? Shall we state it so, and do you agree?

greek_head: Οὐδ' ὁπωστιοῦν Πότερον οὖν τοὺς νέους οὐχ οἷός τ' ἦσθα πείθειν ἐν
greek_tokens: oude opwsoun poteros oun o neos ou oios su eimi peiqw en lakedaimwn ws sos suneimi pleos an eis areth epididwmi h o eautou h o ekeinos pathr adunatew peiqw ostis sos xrh paradidwmi mallon h autos epimeleomai eiper tis o uios khdw ou gar pou fqonew ge o eautou pais ws beltistos gignomai ou oimh egw fqonew alla meis eunomos ge o lakedaimwn pws gar
english_head: Not in the least. Then were you not able to persuade the young men at Lacedaemon that they would make more progress towards virtue by associating with you than with their own people, or were you powerless to persuade their fathers that they ought rather to hand them over to you than to care for them themselves, if they are at all concerned for their sons? For surely they did not begrudge it to their children to become as good as possible. I do not think they begrudged it. But certainly Lacedaemon is well governed. Of course it is.

greek_head: οὔ Ἐν δέ γε ταῖς εὐνόμοις πόλεσιν τιμιώτατον ἡ ἀρετή Πάνυ γε Σὺ δὲ ταύτην παραδιδόναι
greek_tokens: ou en de ge o eunomos polis timios o areth panu ge su de outos paradidwmi allos kallista anqrwpos efisthmi kai polus ge eimi swkraths o oun kallista efisthmi ippikos paradidwmi ara ou an en qessalia o ellas malista timaw kai pleistos xrhma lambanw kai alloqi opou outos spoudazw eoika ge o dh dunamai paradidwmi o pleistos acios maqhma eis areth ou en
english_head: And in well-governed states virtue is most highly honored. Certainly. And you know best of all men how to transmit that to another. Much best, Socrates. Well, he who knows best how to transmit horsemanship would be most honored in Thessaly of all parts of Greece and would receive most money—and anywhere else where horsemanship is a serious interest, would he not? Very likely. Then will not he who is able to transmit the doctrines that are of most value

greek_head: Λακεδαίμονι μάλιστα τιμήσεται καὶ πλεῖστα ἐργάσεται χρήματα ἂν βούληται καὶ ἐν ἄλλῃ πόλει ἥτις τῶν Ἑλληνίδων
greek_tokens: lakedaimwn malista timaw kai pleistos ergazomai xrhma an boulomai kai en allh polis ostis o ellhnis eunomeomai alla en sikelia eimi etairos oiomai mallon kai en inukw outos peiqw eimi ippios ean gar su keleuw peisteon ou gar patrios eimi swkraths lakedaimonios kinew o nomos oude para o eqw paideuw o uios pws legw lakedaimonios ou patrios orqos
english_head: for the acquisition of virtue be most highly honored in Lacedaemon and make most money, if he so wishes, and in any other of the Greek states that is well governed? But do you, my friend, think he will fare better in Sicily and at Inycus? Are we to believe that, Hippias? For if you tell us to do so, we must believe it. Yes, for it is not the inherited usage of the Lacedaemonians to change their laws or to educate their children differently from what is customary. What? For the Lacedaemonians is it the hereditary usage not to act rightly,

greek_head: πράττειν ἀλλ' ἐξαμαρτάνειν Οὐκ ἂν φαίην ἔγωγε ὦ Σώκρατες
greek_tokens: prassw alla ecamartanw ou an fhmi egw eimi swkraths oukoun orqos an prassw beltiwn alla mh xeirwn paideuw o neos orqos alla cenikos paideusis ou nomimos autos paideuw epei eu eimi eiper tis allos ekeiqen xrhma laptw pwpote epi paideusis kai egw an lambanw polus malista xairw goun akouw egw kai epainew alla o legos ou nomos
english_head: but to commit errors? I wouldn’t say so, Socrates. Would they, then, not act rightly in educating the young men better, but not in educating them worse? Yes, they would; but it is not lawful for them to give them a foreign education; for you may be sure that if anybody had ever received money there in payment for education, I should have received by far the most; they certainly enjoy hearing me and they applaud me; but, as I say, it is not the law. But, Hippias, do you say that law is an injury to the state,

greek_head: Νόμον δὲ λέγεις ὦ Ἱππία βλάβην πόλεως εἶναι ἢ ὠφελίαν Τίθεται μὲν οἶμαι ὠφελίας ἕνεκα ἐνίοτε δὲ
greek_tokens: nomos de legw eimi ippios blabh polis eimi h wfeleia tiqhmi men oimh wfeleia eneka eniote de kai blaptw ean kakos tiqhmi o nomos tis de ou ws agaqos megas polis tiqhmi o nomos e tiqhmi kai aneu outos meta eunomia adunatos oikew alhqhs legw otan ara agaqos amartanw e epixeirew o nomos tiqhmi nomimos te kai nomos amartanw h
english_head: or a benefit? It is made, I think, with benefit in view, but sometimes, if the law is badly made, it is injurious. Well, then, is it not true that those who make the law make it as the greatest good to the state, and that without this it is impossible to enjoy good government? What you say is true. Then, when those who make the laws miss the good, they have missed the lawful and the law; or what do you say?

greek_head: πῶς λέγεις Τῷ μὲν ἀκριβεῖ λόγῳ ὦ Σώκρατες οὕτως ἔχει οὐ μέντοι εἰώθασιν ἅνθρωποι ὀνομάζειν οὕτω
greek_tokens: pws legw o men akribhs logos eimi swkraths outws exis ou mentoi eqw anqrwpos onomazw outws poteros eimi ippios e oida h e mh oida e polus eimi de outos e oida alhqhs e polus ou dhta alla meis pou e ge oida o wfelimos o anwfelhs nomimos hgeomai o alhqeia pas anqrwpos h ou sugxwrew nai sugxwrew ostis ge o alhqeia oukoun eimi te kai exis outws ws e oida hgeomai panu ge eimi de ge lakedaimonios ws su fhmi wfelimos
english_head: Speaking accurately, Socrates, that is true; however, men are not accustomed to think so. The men who know, Hippias, or those who do not know? The many. Are these, the many, those who know the truth? Certainly not. But surely those who know, think that in truth for all men that which is more beneficial is more lawful than that which is less beneficial; or do you not agree? Yes, I agree that they think it is so in truth. Well, it actually is as those who know think it is, is it not? Certainly. But or the Lacedaemonians, as you say,

greek_head: τὴν ὑπὸ σοῦ παίδευσιν ξενικὴν οὖσαν παιδεύεσθαι μᾶλλον ἢ τὴν ἐπιχωρίαν Καὶ ἀληθῆ γε λέγω
greek_tokens: o upo sew paideusis cenikos eimi paideuw mallon h o epixwrios kai alhqhs ge legos kai gar ostis o wfelimos nomimos eimi kai outos legw eimi ippios eipon gar kata o sos ara logos o lakedaimonios uios upo ippios paideuw nomimos eimi upo de o pathr anomos eiper o eimi upo sew plew wfelew alla meis wfelew
english_head: it is more beneficial to be educated in your education, which is foreign, than in the local education. Yes, and what I say is true. And do you say this also, Hippias, that beneficial things are more lawful? Yes, I said so. Then, according to what you say, it is more lawful for the sons of the Lacedaemonians to be educated by Hippias and less lawful for them to be educated by their fathers, if in reality they will be more benefited by you. But certainly they will be benefited, Socrates.

greek_head: ὦ Σώκρατες Παρανομοῦσιν ἄρα Λακεδαιμόνιοι οὐ διδόντες σοι χρυσίον καὶ ἐπιτρέποντες τοὺς αὑτῶν
greek_tokens: eimi swkraths paranomew ara lakedaimonios ou didwmi su xrusion kai epitrepw o eautou uios sugxwrew outos dokew gar egw o logos pros egw legw kai oudeis egw deomai autos enantioomai paranomos men dh eimi etairos o lakwn euriskw kai outos eis o megas o nomimos dokew eimi epainew de dh su pros qea eimi ippios kai xairw akouw poios h dhlos dh ostis ekeinos o su kallista
english_head: Then the Lacedaemonians in not giving you money and entrusting their sons to you , act contrary to law. I agree to that; for you seem to be making your argument in my favour, and there is no need of my opposing it. Then my friends, we find that the Lacedaemonians are law-breakers, and that too in the most important affairs—they who are regarded as the most law-abiding of men. But then, for Heaven’s sake, Hippias, what sort of discourses are those for which they applaud you and which they enjoy hearing?

greek_head: ἐπίστασαι τὰ περὶ τὰ ἄστρα τε καὶ τὰ οὐράνια πάθη Οὐδ' ὁπωστιοῦν ταῦτά γε οὐδ' ἀνέχονται Ἀλλὰ
greek_tokens: efisthmi o peri o astron te kai o ouranios paqh oude opwsoun outos ge oude anexw alla peri gewmetria tis xairw akouw oudamos epei oude ariqmew ekeinos ge ws epos eipon polus efisthmi polus ara deon peri ge logismos anexw su epideiknumi polus mentoi nh dios alla dhta ekeinos o su akribhs efisthmi
english_head: Or are they evidently those which you understand most admirably, those about the stars and the phenomena of the heavens? Not in the least; they won’t even endure those. But they enjoy hearing about geometry? Not at all, since one might say that many of them do not even know how to count. Then they are far from enduring a lecture by you on the processes of thought. Far from it indeed, by Zeus. Well, then, those matters which you of all men know best how to discuss,

greek_head: ἀνθρώπων διαιρεῖν περί τε γραμμάτων δυνάμεως καὶ συλλαβῶν καὶ ῥυθμῶν καὶ ἁρμονιῶν Ποίων ὠγαθέ
greek_tokens: anqrwpos diairew peri te gramma dunamis kai sullabh kai ruqmos kai armonia poios agaqos armonia kai gramma alla tis meis eimi o hdews su akroaomai kai epainew autos egw eipon epei egw ou euriskw peri o genos eimi swkraths o te hrws kai o anqrwpos kai o katoikisis ws o arxaios ektinw o polis kai sullhbdhn pas o arxaiologia hdus
english_head: concerning the value of letters and syllables and rhythms and harmonies? Harmonies indeed, my good fellow, and letters! But then what are the things about which they like to listen to you and which they applaud? Tell me yourself, for I cannot discover them. They are very fond of hearing about the genealogies of heroes and men, Socrates, and the foundations of cities in ancient times and, in short, about antiquity in general, so that for their sake I have been obliged to learn all that sort of thing by heart

greek_head: ἀκροῶνται ὥστ' ἔγωγε δι' αὐτοὺς ἠνάγκασμαι ἐκμεμαθηκέναι τε καὶ ἐκμεμελετηκέναι πάντα τὰ τοιαῦτα
greek_tokens: akroaomai wste egw dia autos anagkazw ekmanqanw te kai ekmeletaw pas o toioutos nai ma diw eimi ippios eutuxew ge ostis lakedaimonios ou xairw an tis autos apo solwn o arxw o hmeteros katalegw ei de mh pragma an exw ekmanqanw pinw eimi swkraths apac aekwn penthkonta onoma apomnhmoneuw alhqhs legw alla egw ou ennoew ostis o mnhmonikos exis wste ennoew ostis eoikotws su xairw
english_head: and practise it thoroughly. english_head: Well, then, he will say, do you imagine that Pheidias did not know this beautiful that you speak of? Why do you ask that? I shall say. Because, he will say, he did not make the eyes of his Athena of gold, nor the rest of her face, nor her hands and feet, if, that is, they were sure to appear most beautiful provided only they were made of gold, but he made them of ivory; evidently he made this mistake through ignorance, not knowing that it is gold which makes everything beautiful to which it is added. When he says that, what reply shall we make to him, Hippias?

greek_head: Οὐδὲν χαλεπόν ἐροῦμεν γὰρ ὅτι ὀρθῶς ἐποίησε καὶ γὰρ τὸ ἐλεφάντινον οἶμαι καλόν ἐστιν
greek_tokens: oudeis xalepos erew gar ostis orqos poiew kai gar o elefantinos oimh kalos eimi o oun eneka fhmi ou kai o mesh o ofqalmos elefantinos ergazomai alla liqinos ws oios su eimi omoioths o liqos o elefas eceuriskw h kai o liqos o kalos kalos eimi fhmi eimi ippios fhmi mentoi otan ge prepw eimi otan de mh prepw aisxros omologew h mh omologew otan ge mh prepw
english_head: That is easy; for we shall say that Pheidias did right; for ivory, I think, is beautiful. Why, then, he will say, did he not make the middle parts of the eyes also of ivory, but of stone, procuring stone as similar as possible to the ivory? Or is beautiful stone also beautiful? Shall we say that it is, Hippias? Surely we shall say so, that is, where it is appropriate. But ugly when not appropriate? Shall I agree, or not? Agree, that is, when it is not appropriate.

greek_head: Τί δὲ δή ὁ ἐλέφας καὶ ὁ χρυσός φήσει ὦ σοφὲ σύ οὐχ ὅταν μὲν πρέπῃ καλὰ ποιεῖ φαίνεσθαι ὅταν
greek_tokens: tis de dh o elefas kai o xrusos fhmi eimi sofos su ou otan men prepw kalos poiew fainw otan de mh aisxros ecarnos eimi h omologew autos orqos legw autos omologew outos ge ostis o an prepw ekastos outos kalos poiew ekastos poteros oun prepw fhmi otan tis o xutra os arti legw o kalos epomai etnos kalew mestos xruseos torunh autos h sukinos hraklehs oios legw anqrwpos eimi swkraths ou
english_head: What then? Do not gold and ivory, he will say, when they are appropriate, make things beautiful, and when they are not appropriate, ugly? Shall we deny that, or agree that what he says is correct? We shall agree to this, at any rate, that whatever is appropriate to any particular thing makes that thing beautiful. Well, then, he will say, when some one has boiled the pot of which we were speaking just now, the beautiful one, full of beautiful soup, is a golden ladle appropriate to it, or one made of fig wood? Heracles! What a fellow this is that you speak of!

greek_head: βούλει μοι εἰπεῖν τίς ἐστιν Οὐ γὰρ ἂν γνοίης εἴ σοι εἴποιμι τοὔνομα
greek_tokens: boulomai egw eipon tis eimi ou gar an gignwskw ei su eipon onoma alla kai nun egw gignwskw ostis amaqhs tis eimi mermeros panu eimi eimi ippios alla omws tis fhmi poteros prepw o torunh o etnos kai o xutra h dhlos ostis o sukinos euwdhs gar pou o etnos poiew kai ama eimi etairos ou an suntribw egw o xutra ekxew o etnos kai o pur aposbennumi kai o mellw estiaw aneu oyon an panu gennaios poiew o de xruseos ekeinos pas an outos poiew wste egw
english_head: Won’t you tell me who he is? You would not know him if I should tell you his name. But even now I know that he is an ignoramus. He is a great nuisance, Hippias but yet, what shall we say? Which of the two ladles shall we say is appropriate to the soup and the pot? Is it not evidently the one of fig wood? For it is likely to make the soup smell better, and besides, my friend, it would not break the pot, thereby spilling the soup, putting out the fire, and making those who are to be entertained go without their splendid soup; whereas the golden ladle would do all those things,

greek_head: δοκεῖν τὴν συκίνην ἡμᾶς μᾶλλον φάναι πρέπειν ἢ τὴν χρυσῆν εἰ μή τι σὺ ἄλλο λέγεις
greek_tokens: dokew o sukinos egw mallon fainw prepw h o xruseos ei mh tis su allos legw prepw men gar eimi swkraths mallon ou mentoi egw o anqrwpos toioutos erwtaw dialegw orqos ge eimi filos sos men gar ou an prepw toioutos onoma anapimplhmi kalos men outwsi ampexw kalos de upodew eudokimew de epi sofia en pas o ellhn alla egw oudeis pragma furw
english_head: so that it seems to me that we must say that the wooden ladle is more appropriate than the golden one, unless you disagree. No, for it is more appropriate, Socrates; however, I, for my part, would not talk with the fellow when he asks such questions. Quite right, my friend; for it would not be appropriate for you to be filled up with such words, you who are so beautifully clad, so beautifully shod, and so famous for your wisdom among all the Greeks; but for me it doesn’t matter if I do associate with the fellow;

greek_head: πρὸς τὸν ἄνθρωπον ἐμὲ οὖν προδίδασκε καὶ ἐμὴν χάριν ἀποκρίνου Εἰ γὰρ δὴ πρέπει γε μᾶλλον ἡ συκίνη τῆς
greek_tokens: pros o anqrwpos egw oun prodidaskw kai emos xaris apokrinw ei gar dh prepw ge mallon o sukinos o xruseos fhmi o anqrwpos allos tis kai kallos an eaw epei o prepw eimi swkraths kallion omologew eimi o mh prepw allos tis omologew eimi ippios o sukinos kallias o xruseos eimi boulomai su eipon eimi swkraths o eipon eimi o kalos apallacis sautou o polus logos
english_head: so instruct me and for my sake answer him. For if the wooden one is more appropriate than the golden one, the fellow will say, would it not be more beautiful, since you agreed, Socrates, that the appropriate is more beautiful than that which is not appropriate? Shall we not agree, Hippias, that the wooden one is more beautiful than the golden? Do you wish me to tell you, Socrates, what definition of the beautiful will enable you to free yourself from long discussion?

greek_head: Πάνυ μὲν οὖν μὴ μέντοι πρότερόν γε πρὶν ἄν μοι εἴπῃς ποτέραν ἀποκρίνωμαι οἷν ἄρτι ἔλεγον τοῖν τορύναιν

english_head: Certainly; but not until after you have told me which of the two ladles I just spoke of I shall reply is appropriate and more beautiful. Well, if you like, reply to him that it is the one made of fig wood. Now, then, say what you were just now going to say. For by this reply, if I say that the beautiful is gold, it seems to me that gold will be shown to be no more beautiful than fig wood; but what do you now, once more, say that the beautiful is?

greek_head: Ἐγώ σοι ἐρῶ ζητεῖν γάρ μοι δοκεῖς τοιοῦτόν τι τὸ καλὸν ἀποκρίνασθαι ὃ μηδέποτε αἰσχρὸν μηδαμοῦ μηδενὶ
greek_tokens: egw su eraomai zhtew gar egw dokew toioutos tis o kalos apokrinw o mhdepote aisxros mhdamos mhdeis fainw panu men oun eimi ippios kai kalos ge nun upolambanw akouw dh pros gar outos eimi ean tis exis ostis anteipon fainw egw mhde otioun epaiw legos dh ws taxistos pros qea legos toinun aei kai pas kai pantaxou kalos eimi andris ploutew ugiainw timaw upo o ellhn afikneomai eis ghras o eautou goneus teleutaw
english_head: I will tell you; for you seem to me to be seeking to reply that the beautiful is something of such sort that it will never appear ugly anywhere to anybody. Certainly, Hippias; now you understand beautifully. Listen, then; for, mind you, if anyone has anything to say against this, you may say I know nothing at all. Then for Heaven’s sake, speak as quickly as you can. I say, then, that for every man and everywhere it is most beautiful to be rich and healthy, and honored by the Greeks, to reach old age, and, after providing a beautiful funeral for his deceased parents,

greek_head: καλῶς περιστείλαντι ὑπὸ τῶν αὑτοῦ ἐκγόνων καλῶς καὶ μεγαλοπρεπῶς ταφῆναι
greek_tokens: kalos peristellw upo o eautou ekgonos kalos kai megaloprephs qaptw iou iou eimi ippios eimi qaumasios te kai megaleios kai acios sautou erw kai nh o hra agamai su ostis egw dokew eunoikos kaqa osos oios su ei bohqew alla gar o anhr ou tugxanw alla egw dh nun kai pleistos katagelaw eu eimi ponhros ge eimi swkraths gelws otan gar pros outos exis men mhdeis ostis legos gelaw de eautou katagelaw
english_head: to be beautifully and splendidly buried by his own offspring. Bravo, bravo, Hippias! You have spoken in a way that is wonderful and great and worthy of you; and now, by Hera, I thank you, because you are kindly coming to my assistance to the best of your ability. But our shots are not hitting the man; no, he will laugh at us now more than ever, be sure of that. A wretched laugh, Socrates; for when he has nothing to say to this, but laughs, he will be laughing at himself

greek_head: καὶ ὑπὸ τῶν παρόντων αὐτὸς ἔσται καταγέλαστος Ἴσως οὕτως ἔχει ἴσως μέντοι ἐπί γε ταύτῃ τῇ
greek_tokens: kai upo o pareimi autos eimi katagelastos isos outws exis isos mentoi epi ge outos o apokrisls ws egw manteuomai kinduneuw ou monos egw katagelaw alla tis meis ostis an tuxh bakthria exw an mh ekfeugw feugw autos eu mala egw efikneomai peiraw pws legw despoths tis su o anqrwpos eimi kai outos poiew ou agw kai dikh oflhsis h ou
english_head: and will himself be laughed at by those present. Perhaps that is so perhaps, however, after this reply, he will, I foresee, be likely to do more than laugh at me. Why do you say that, pray? Because, if he happens to have a stick, unless I get away in a hurry, he will try to fetch me a good one. What? Is the fellow some sort of master of yours, and if he does that, will he not be arrested and have to pay for it? Or does your city disregard justice

greek_head: ἔνδικος ὑμῖν ἡ πόλις ἐστίν ἀλλ' ἐᾷ ἀδίκως τύπτειν ἀλλήλους τοὺς πολίτας
greek_tokens: endikos su o polis eimi alla eaw adikos tuptw allhlwn o poliths oude opwsoun eaw oukoun didwmi dikh adikos ge su tuptw ou egw dokew eimi ippios ou ei outos ge apokrinw alla dikaios egw dokew kai egw toinun dokew eimi swkraths epei ge autos outos oiomai oukoun eipon su kai h autos oiomai dikaios an tuptw outos apokrinw h kai su egw akritos tuptw h decis logos
english_head: and allow the citizens to beat one another unjustly? Oh no that is not allowed at all. Then he will have to pay a penalty for beating you unjustly. I do not think so, Hippias. No, if I were to make that reply, the beating would be just, I think. Then I think so, too, Socrates, since that is your own belief. Shall I, then, not tell you why it is my own belief that the beating would be just, if I made that reply? Or will you also beat me without trial? Or will you listen to what I have to say?

greek_head: Δεινὸν γὰρ ἂν εἴη ὦ Σώκρατες εἰ μὴ δεχοίμην ἀλλὰ πῶς λέγεις
greek_tokens: deinos gar an eaw eimi swkraths ei mh dexomai alla pws legw egw su eraomai o autos tropos os nundi mimeomai ekeinos ina mh pros sos legos rhma oios ekeinos eis egw eraomai xalepos te kai allokotos eu gar eimi eipon egw fhmi eimi swkraths oiomai an adikos plhgas lambanw ostis diqurambos tosoutos aeidw outws amousos polus apadw apo o erwthma pws dh fhmi egw opws fhmi ou oios su ei mimnhskw ostis o kalos autos erwtaw
english_head: It would be shocking if I would not listen; but what have you to say? I will tell you, imitating him in the same way as a while ago, that I may not use to you such harsh and uncouth words as he uses to me. For you may be sure, Tell me, Socrates, he will say, do you think it would be unjust if you got a beating for singing such a long dithyramb so unmusically and so far from the question? How so? I shall say. How so? he will say; are you not able to remember that I asked for the absolute beautiful,

greek_head: ὃ παντὶ ᾧ ἂν προσγένηται ὑπάρχει ἐκείνῳ καλῷ εἶναι καὶ λίθῳ καὶ ξύλῳ καὶ ἀνθρώπῳ καὶ θεῷ καὶ πάσῃ πράξει
greek_tokens: o pas os an prosgignomai uparxw ekeinos kalos eimi kai liqos kai culon kai anqrwpos kai qeaw kai paomai prassw kai pas maqhma autos gar egw anqrwpos kallos erwtaw ostis eimi kai oudeis su mallon gegwnew dunamai h ei egw parhmai liqos kai outos mulios mhte ous mhte egkefalos exw ei oun fobew eipon egw epi outos ode ara ou an axqomai eimi ippios alla mentoi ode o
english_head: by which everything to which it is added has the property of being beautiful, both stone and stick and man and god and every act and every acquisition of knowledge? For what I am asking is this, man: what is absolute beauty? and I cannot make you hear what I say any more than if you were a stone sitting beside me, and a millstone at that, having neither ears nor brain. Would you, then, not be angry, Hippias,if I should be frightened and should reply in this way? Well, but Hippias said that this was the beautiful;

greek_head: καλὸν εἶναι Ἱππίας ἔφη καίτοι ἐγὼ αὐτὸν ἠρώτων οὕτως ὥσπερ σὺ ἐμέ ὃ πᾶσι καλὸν καὶ ἀεί ἐστι πῶς οὖν φῄς
greek_tokens: kalos eimi ippios fhmi kaitoi egw autos erwtaw outws wsper su egw o pas kalos kai aei eimi pws oun fhmi ou axqomai an eipon outos eu ge oun oida eimi swkraths ostis pas kalos outos eimi o egw eipon kai docis eimi kai eimi fhmi aei gar pou o ge kalos kalos panu ge oukoun kai eimi fhmi kai eimi eimi kai o axilleus fhmi o cenos o hleios fhmi kalos eimi usteron o progonos qaptw kai o pappos autos aiakos kai o allos osos
english_head: and yet I asked him, just as you asked me, what is beautiful to all and always. What do you say? Will you not be angry if I say that? I know very well, Socrates, that this which I said was beautiful is beautiful to all and will seem so. And will it be so, too he will say for the beautiful is always beautiful, is it not? Certainly. Then was it so, too? he will say. It was so, too. And, he will say, did the stranger from Elis say also that for Achilles it was beautiful to be buried later than his parents, and for his grandfather Aeacus, and all the others who were born of gods,

greek_head: ἐκ θεῶν γεγόνασι καὶ αὐτοῖς τοῖς θεοῖς Τί τοῦτο βάλλ' ἐς μακαρίαν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου οὐδ'
greek_tokens: ek qea gignomai kai autos o qeaw tis outos ballis eis makarios o anqrwpos oude eufhmos eimi swkraths outos ge o erwthma tis de o eromai eteros fainw outos outws exw ou panu dusfhmos isos isos toinun su ei outos fhmi os pas fhmi kai aei kalos eimi upo men o ekgonos qaptw o de goneus qaptw h ou eis o apas kai hraklehs eimi kai os nundi legw pas alla ou o qeaw egw elegos
english_head: and for the gods themselves? What’s that? Confound it! These questions of the fellow’s are not even respectful to religion. Well, then, when another asks the question, perhaps it is not quite disrespectful to religion to say that these things are so? Perhaps. Perhaps, then, you are the man, he will say, who says that it is beautiful for every one and always to be buried by one’s offspring, and to bury one’s parents; or was not Heracles included in ’every one,’ he and all those whom we just now mentioned? But I did not say it was so for the gods. Nor for the heroes either, apparently.

greek_head: Οὐδὲ τοῖς ἥρωσιν ὡς ἔοικας Οὐχ ὅσοι γε θεῶν παῖδες ἦσαν
greek_tokens: oude o hrws ws eoika ou osos ge qea pais eimi alla osos mh panu ge oukoun kata o sos au logos ws fainw o hrws o men tantalos kai o dardanos kai o zhqos deinos te kai anosios kai aisxros eimi peloy de kai o allos o outws gignomai kalos egw dokew sos toinun dokew fhmi o arti ou fhmi o qaptw o progonos qaptw upo o ekgonos eniote kai
english_head: Not those who were children of gods. But those who were not? Certainly. Then again, according to your statement, among the heroes it is terrible and impious and disgraceful for Tantalus and Dardanus and Zethus, but beautiful for Pelops and the others who were born as he was? I think so. You think, then, what you did not say just now, that to bury one’s parents and be buried by one’s offspring is sometimes and for some persons disgraceful;

greek_head: ἐνίοις αἰσχρὸν εἶναι ἔτι δὲ μᾶλλον ὡς ἔοικεν ἀδύνατον πᾶσι τοῦτο γενέσθαι καὶ εἶναι καλόν ὥστε τοῦτό γε ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: eneimi aisxros eimi eti de mallon ws eoika adunatos pas outos gignomai kai eimi kalos wste outos ge wsper kai o emprosqen ekeinos o te parqenos kai o xutra tautos pasxw kai eti geloios o men eimi kalos o de ou kalos kai oudepw kai shmeron fhmi oios su ei eimi swkraths peri o kalew ostis eimi o erwtaw apokrinw outos egw kai toioutos oneidizw dikaios ean autos outws apokrinw o men oun polus eimi ippios sxedon
english_head: and it is still more impossible, as it seems, for this to become and to be beautiful for all, so that the same thing has happened to this as to the things we mentioned before, the maiden and the pot, in a still more ridiculous way than to them; it is beautiful for some and not beautiful for others. And you are not able yet, even today, Socrates, he will say, to answer what is asked about the beautiful, namely what it is. With these words and the like he will rebuke me, if I reply to him in this way.

greek_head: τί μοι οὕτω διαλέγεται ἐνίοτε δὲ ὥσπερ ἐλεήσας μου τὴν ἀπειρίαν καὶ ἀπαιδευσίαν αὐτός μοι προβάλλει ἐρωτῶν εἰ
greek_tokens: tis egw outws dialegw eniote de wsper eleaw egw o apeiria kai apaideusia autos egw proballw erwtaw ei toiosde egw dokew eimi o kalos h kai peri allos ostis an tuxh punqanomai kai peri e an logos eimi pws outos legw eimi swkraths egw su frazw eimi daimonios fhmi swkraths o men toioutos apokrinw kai outws pauw lian gar euhqhs te kai euecelegktos eimi alla o toiosde
english_head: For the most part, Hippias, he talks with me in some such way as that but sometimes, as if in pity for my inexperience and lack of training, he himself volunteers a question, and asks whether I think the beautiful is so and so or whatever else it is which happens to be the subject of our questions and our discussion. What do you mean by that, Socrates? I will tell you. Oh, my dear Socrates, he says, stop making replies of this sort and in this way—for they are too silly and easy to refute; but see if something like this does not seem to you to be beautiful,

greek_head: σκόπει εἴ σοι δοκεῖ καλὸν εἶναι οὗ καὶ νυνδὴ ἐπελαβόμεθα ἐν τῇ ἀποκρίσει ἡνίκ' ἔφαμεν τὸν χρυσὸν οἷς μὲν πρέπει
greek_tokens: skopaw ei su dokew kalos eimi e kai nundi epilambanw en o apokrisls hnika fhmi o xrusos os men prepw kalos eimi os de mh ou kai allos pas os an outos proseimi autos dh outos o prepw kai o fus autos o prepw skopaw ei outos tugxanw eimi o kalos egw men oun eqw sumfhmi o toioutos ekastote ou gar exw ostis legos sos de oun dokew o prepw kalos eimi pantws dhpou eimi swkraths skopaw mh ph ara ecapataw alla xrh skopaw oraw toinun o prepw ara outos legw o paragignomai
english_head: which we got hold of just now in our reply, when we said that gold was beautiful for those things for which it was appropriate, but not for those for which it was not, and that all the other things were beautiful to which this quality pertains; so examine this very thing, the appropriate, and see if it is perchance the beautiful. Now I am accustomed to agree to such things every time for I don’t know what to say; but now does it seem to you that the appropriate is the beautiful? Yes, certainly, Socrates. Let us consider, lest we make a mistake somehow. Yes, we must consider. See, then; do we say that the appropriate is that which,

greek_head: ποιεῖ ἕκαστα φαίνεσθαι καλὰ τούτων οἷς ἂν παρῇ ἢ ὃ εἶναι ποιεῖ ἢ οὐδέτερα τούτων
greek_tokens: poiew ekastos fainw kalos outos os an pareimi h o eimi poiew h oudeteros outos egw dokew poteros o poiew fainw kalos wsper ge epeidan imation tis lambanw h upodhma armozw kan eimi geloios kallos fainw oukoun eiper kallias poiew fainw h eimi o prepw apath tis an eaw peri o kalos o prepw kai ou an eaw outos o egw zhtew eimi ippios egw men gar pou
english_head: when it is added, makes each of those things to which it is added appear beautiful, or which makes them be beautiful, or neither of these? I think so. Which? That which makes them appear beautiful; as when a man takes clothes or shoes that fit, even if he be ridiculous, he appears more beautiful. Then if the appropriate makes him appear more beautiful than he is, the appropriate would be a sort of deceit in respect to the beautiful, and would not be that which we are looking for, would it, Hippias?

greek_head: ἐκεῖνο ἐζητοῦμεν ᾧ πάντα τὰ καλὰ πράγματα καλά ἐστιν ὥσπερ ᾧ πάντα τὰ μεγάλα ἐστὶ μεγάλα τῷ ὑπερέχοντι
greek_tokens: ekeinos zhtew os pas o kalos pragma kalos eimi wsper os pas o megas eimi megas o uperexw outos gar pas megas eimi kai ean mh fainw uperexw de anagkh autos megas eimi outws dh fhmi kai o kalos os kalos pas eimi anta oun fainw antomai mh tis an eaw o men gar prepw ou an eaw kallias gar poiew fainw h eimi ws o sos logos oios de eimi ou eaw fainw o de poiew eimi kalos osper nundi eipon
english_head: For we were rather looking for that by which all beautiful things are beautiful—like that by which all great things are great, that is, excess; for it is by this that all great things are great; for even if they do not appear great, but exceed, they are of necessity great; so, then, we say, what would the beautiful be, by which all things are beautiful, whether they appear so or not? For it could not be the appropriate, since that, by your statement, makes things appear more beautiful than they are, but does not let them appear such as they are.

greek_head: ἐάντε φαίνηται ἐάντε μή πειρατέον λέγειν τί ἐστι τοῦτο γὰρ ζητοῦμεν εἴπερ τὸ καλὸν ζητοῦμεν
greek_tokens: ean fainw ean mh peirateon legw tis eimi outos gar zhtew eiper o kalos zhtew alla o prepw eimi swkraths kai eimi kai fainw poiew kalos pareimi adunatos ara o eimi kalos eimi mh fainw kalos eimi pareimi ge o poiew fainw adunatos omologew oun outos eimi ippios pas o o eimi kalos kai nomimos kai epithdeuma kai docazw kalos
english_head: But we must try to say what that is which makes things be beautiful, as I said just now, whether they appear so or not; for that is what we are looking for, since we are looking for the beautiful. But the appropriate, Socrates, makes things both be and appear beautiful by its presence. Is it impossible, then, for things which are really beautiful not to appear to be beautiful, at any rate when that is present which makes them appear so? It is impossible. Shall we, then, agree to this, Hippias, that all things which are really beautiful, both uses and pursuits, are always believed to be beautiful by all, and appear so to them,

greek_head: εἶναι καὶ φαίνεσθαι ἀεὶ πᾶσιν ἢ πᾶν τοὐναντίον ἀγνοεῖσθαι καὶ πάντων μάλιστα ἔριν καὶ μάχην περὶ αὐτῶν εἶναι καὶ
greek_tokens: eimi kai fainw aei pas h pas enantion agnoew kai pas malista eris kai maxh peri autos eimi kai idios ekastos kai dhmosios o polis outws mallon eimi swkraths agnoew ou an ei ge pou o fainw autos proseimi proseimi de an eiper o prepw kalos eimi kai mh monos kalos poiew eimi alla kai fainw wste o prepw ei men o kalos poiew eimi eimi o men kalos an eaw o egw zhtew ou mentoi o ge poiew fainw ei de au o
english_head: or, quite the contrary, that people are ignorant about them, and that there is more strife and contention about them than about anything else, both in private between individuals and in public between states? The latter rather, Socrates; that people are ignorant about them. They would not be so, if the appearance of beauty were added to them; and it would be added, if the appropriate were beautiful and made things not only to be beautiful, but also to appear so. So that the appropriate, if it is that which makes things be beautiful, would be the beautiful which we are looking for, but would not be that which makes things appear beautiful; but if, on the other hand, the appropriate is that which makes things appear beautiful,

greek_head: φαίνεσθαι ποιοῦν ἐστιν τὸ πρέπον οὐκ ἂν εἴη τὸ καλόν ὃ ἡμεῖς ζητοῦμεν εἶναι γὰρ ἐκεῖνό γε ποιεῖ φαίνεσθαι δὲ καὶ
greek_tokens: fainw poiew eimi o prepw ou an eaw o kalos o egw zhtew eimi gar ekeinos ge poiew fainw de kai poiew eimi ou monos kalos ou an pote dunamai o autos alla oude allos otioun airew dh poteros dokew o prepw eimi o fainw kalos poiew h o eimi o fainw egw dokew eimi swkraths babai oixomai ara egw diafeugw eimi ippios o kalos gignwskw ostis pote eimi epei ge o prepw allos tis fainw eimi h kalos nai ma dios eimi swkraths kai mala egw atopos
english_head: it would not be the beautiful for which we are looking. For that makes things be beautiful, but the same element could not make things both appear and be beautiful, nor could it make them both appear and be anything else whatsoever. Let us choose, then, whether we think that the appropriate is that which makes things appear or be beautiful. That which makes them appear so, in my opinion, Socrates. Whew! Our perception of what the beautiful is has fled away and gone, Hippias, since the appropriate has been found to be something other than the beautiful. Yes, by Zeus, Socrates, and to me that is very queer.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μέντοι ὦ ἑταῖρε μήπω γε ἀνῶμεν αὐτό ἔτι γάρ τινα ἐλπίδα ἔχω ἐκφανήσεσθαι τί ποτ' ἐστὶν τὸ καλόν
greek_tokens: alla mentoi eimi etairos mhpw ge anazw autos eti gar tis elpis exw ekfainw tis pote eimi o kalos pantws dhpou eimi swkraths oude gar xalepos eimi euriskw egw men oun eu oida ostis ei oligos xronos eis erhmia erxomai skeptomai pros emautou akribhs an autos su eipon o apas akribeia a mh megas eimi ippios legos oraw osos pragma egw hdh parexw mh kai orgizw egw eti mallon
english_head: However, my friend, let us not yet give it up, for I still have hopes that what the beautiful is will be made clear. Certainly, to be sure, Socrates, for it is not hard to find. Now I know that if I should go away into solitude and meditate alone by myself, I could tell it to you with the most perfect accuracy. Ah, don’t boast, Hippias. You see how much trouble it has caused us already; I’m afraid it may get angry and run away more than ever.

greek_head: ἀποδρᾷ καίτοι οὐδὲν λέγω σὺ μὲν γὰρ οἶμαι ῥᾳδίως αὐτὸ εὑρήσεις ἐπειδὰν μόνος γένῃ ἀλλὰ πρὸς θεῶν ἐμοῦ ἐναντίον
greek_tokens: apodraw kaitoi oudeis legos su men gar oimh radios autos eurhsis epeidan monos gignomai alla pros qea egw enantion autos eceuriskw ei de boulomai wsper nun egw suzhtew kai ean men euriskw kallista ecis ei de mh stergw oimh egw o emew tuxh su de aperxomai radios eurhsis kai ean men nun euriskw amelei ou oxlhros eimi su punqanomai ostis eimi ekeinos o kata sautou eceuriskw nun de qeaomai autos o su
english_head: And yet that is nonsense; for you, I think, will easily find it when you go away by yourself. But for Heaven’s sake, find it in my presence, or, if you please, join me, as you are now doing, in looking for it. And if we find it, that will be splendid, but if we do not, I shall, I suppose, accept my lot, and you will go away and find it easily. And if we find it now, I shall certainly not be a nuisance to you by asking what that was which you found by yourself;

greek_head: δοκεῖ εἶναι τὸ καλόν λέγω δὴ αὐτὸ εἶναι ἀλλὰ γὰρ ἐπισκόπει μοι πάνυ προσέχων τὸν νοῦν μὴ παραληρήσω τοῦτο
greek_tokens: dokew eimi o kalos legos dh autos eimi alla gar episkopew egw panu prosexw o new mh paralhrew outos gar dh eimi egw kalos o an xrhsimos eimi eipon de ek ode ennoew kalos fhmi e ofqalmos eimi ou e an dokazw toioutos eimi oios mh dunatos oraw alla e an dunatos te kai xrhsimos pros o eidon eimi gar nai oukoun kai o oloc swma outws legw kalos eimi o men pros dromos o de pros pala kai au o
english_head: but now once more see if this is in your opinion the beautiful : I say, then, that it is—but consider, paying close attention to me, that I may not talk nonsense—for I say, then, whatever is useful shall be for us beautiful. But I said it with this reason for my thought; beautiful eyes, we say, are not such as seem to be so, which are unable to see, but those which are able and useful for seeing. Is that right? Yes. Then, too, in the same way we say that the whole body is beautiful, part of it for running, part for wrestling;

greek_head: ζῷα πάντα ἵππον καλὸν καὶ ἀλεκτρυόνα καὶ ὄρτυγα καὶ τὰ σκεύη πάντα καὶ τὰ ὀχήματα τά τε πεζὰ καὶ τὰ ἐν τῇ
greek_tokens: zwion pas ippos kalos kai alektruwn kai ortuc kai o skeuos pas kai o oxhma o te pezos kai o en o qalassa ploion te kai trihrhs kai o ge organon pas o te upo o mousikh kai o upo o allos texnh ei de boulomai o epithdeuma kai o nomos sxedon tis pas outos kalos prosagoreuw o autos tropos apoblepw pros ekastos autos h fuw h ergazomai h kew o men xrhsimos kai h xrhsimos kai pros o xrhsimos
english_head: and again all the animals, a beautiful horse or cock or quail and all utensils and land vehicles, and on the sea freight-ships and ships of war; and all instruments in music and in the other arts, and, if you like, customs and laws also—pretty well all these we call beautiful in the same way looking at each of them—how it is formed by nature, how it is wrought, how it has been enacted—the useful we call beautiful, and beautiful in the way in which it is useful, and for the purpose for which it is useful, and at the time when it is useful;

greek_head: καὶ ὁπότε χρήσιμον καλόν φαμεν εἶναι τὸ δὲ ταύτῃ πάντῃ ἄχρηστον αἰσχρόν ἆρ' οὐ καὶ σοὶ δοκεῖ οὕτως ὦ
greek_tokens: kai opote xrhsimos kalos fhmi eimi o de outos panth axrhstos aisxros ara ou kai sos dokew outws eimi ippios egw orqos ara nun legw ostis tugxanw pas eimi mallon kalos o xrhsimos orqos mentoi eimi swkraths oukoun o dunatos ekastos apergazomai eis osper dunatos eis outos kai xrhsimos o de adunatos axrhstos panu ge dunamis men ara kalos adunamia de aisxros sfodra ge o te goun allos
english_head: and that which is in all these aspects useless we say is ugly. Now is not this your opinion also, Hippias? It is.Soc, Then are we right in saying that the useful rather than everything else is beautiful? We are right, surely, Socrates. Now that which has power to accomplish anything is useful for that for which it has power, but that which is powerless is useless, is it not? Certainly. Power, then, is beautiful, and want of power is disgraceful or ugly. Decidedly. Now other things, Socrates,

greek_head: ὦ Σώκρατες μαρτυρεῖ ἡμῖν ὅτι τοῦτο οὕτως ἔχει ἀτὰρ οὖν καὶ τὰ πολιτικά ἐν γὰρ τοῖς πολιτικοῖς τε καὶ τῇ ἑαυτοῦ
greek_tokens: eimi swkraths marturomai egw ostis outos outws exis atar oun kai o politikos en gar o politikos te kai o eautou polis o men dunatos eimi pas kalos o de adunatos pas aisxros eu legw ara oun pros qea ippios dia outos kai o sofia pas kalos o de amaqia pas aisxros alla tis oiomai eimi swkraths exw dh hrema eimi filos etairos ws fobew tis pote au legw
english_head: testify for us that this is so, but especially political affairs; for in political affairs and in one’s own state to be powerful is the most beautiful of all things, but to be powerless is the most disgraceful of all. Good! Then, for Heaven’s sake, Hippias, is wisdom also for this reason the most beautiful of all things and ignorance the most disgraceful of all things? Well, what do you suppose, Socrates? Just keep quiet, my dear friend; I am so afraid and wondering what in the world we are saying again.

greek_head: Τί δ' αὖ φοβῇ ὦ Σώκρατες ἐπεὶ νῦν γέ σοι ὁ λόγος παγκάλως προβέβηκε
greek_tokens: tis de au fobew eimi swkraths epei nun ge su o logos pagkalos probainw boulomai an alla egw ode sunepiskeptomai ara an tis tis poiew o mhte efisthmi mhte o parapan dunamai oudamos pws gar an o ge mh dunamai e oun ecamartanw kai kakos ergazomai te kai poiew akwn allos tis outos ei mh dunamai outos poiew ou an pote poiew dhlos dh alla mentoi dunamis
english_head: What are you afraid of again, Socrates, since now your discussion has gone ahead most beautifully? I wish that might be the case; but consider this point with me: could a person do what he did not know how and was utterly powerless to do? By no means; for how could he do what he was powerless to do? Then those who commit errors and accomplish and do bad things involuntarily, if they were powerless to do those things, would not do them?

greek_head: γε δύνανται οἱ δυνάμενοι οὐ γάρ που ἀδυναμίᾳ γε Οὐ δῆτα Δύνανται δέ γε πάντες ποιεῖν οἱ ποιοῦντες
greek_tokens: ge dunamai e dunamai ou gar pou adunamia ge ou dhta dunamai de ge pas poiew e poiew o poiew nai kakos de ge polus plew poiew h agaqos pas anqrwpos arxw ek pais kai ecamartanw akwn eimi outos tis oun outos o dunamis kai outos o xrhsimos o an eimi epi o kakos tis ergazomai xrhsimos ara fhmi outos eimi
english_head: Evidently not. But yet it is by power that those are powerful who are powerful for surely it is not by powerlessness. Certainly not. And all who do, have power to do what they do? Yes. Men do many more bad things than good, from childhood up, and commit many errors involuntarily. That is true. Well, then, this power and these useful things, which are useful for accomplishing something bad—shall we say that they are beautiful, or far from it?

greek_head: καλά ἢ πολλοῦ δεῖ Πολλοῦ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες Οὐκ ἄρα ὦ Ἱππία τὸ δυνατόν τε καὶ τὸ
greek_tokens: kalos h polus deomai polus egw dokew eimi swkraths ou ara eimi ippios o dunatos te kai o xrhsimos egw ws eoika eimi o kalos ean ge eimi swkraths agaqos dunamai kai epi toioutos xrhsimos eimi ekeinos men toinun oixomai o dunatos te kai xrhsimos aplos eimi kalos alla ara outos eimi ekeinos eimi ippios o boulomai egw o yuxh eipon ostis o xrhsimos te kai o dunatos epi o agaqos tis poiew outos eimi o
english_head: Far from it, in my opinion, Socrates. Then, Hippias, the powerful and the useful are not, as it seems, our beautiful. They are, Socrates, if they are powerful and useful for good. Then that assertion, that the powerful and useful are beautiful without qualification, is gone; but was this, Hippias, what our soul wished to say, that the useful and the powerful for doing something good is the beautiful?

greek_head: καλόν Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ Ἀλλὰ μὴν τοῦτό γε ὠφέλιμόν ἐστιν ἢ οὔ Πάνυ γε Οὕτω δὴ
greek_tokens: kalos egw dokew alla meis outos ge wfelimos eimi h ou panu ge outws dh kai o kalos swma kai o kalos nomimos kai o sofia kai o nundi legw pas kalos eimi ostis wfelimos dhlos ostis o wfelimos ara eoika egw eimi o kalos eimi ippios pantws dhpou eimi swkraths alla meis o ge wfelimos o poiew agaqos eimi eimi gar o poiew de ge eimi ou allos tis h o aitios eimi gar outws o agaqos ara
english_head: Yes, in my opinion. But surely this is beneficial; or is it not? Certainly. So by this argument the beautiful persons and beautiful customs and all that we mentioned just now are beautiful because they are beneficial. Evidently. Then the beneficial seems to us to be the beautiful, Hippias. Yes, certainly, Socrates. But the beneficial is that which creates good. Yes, it is. But that which creates is nothing else than the cause; am I right? It is so. Then the beautiful is the cause of the good.

greek_head: αἴτιόν ἐστιν τὸ καλόν Ἔστι γάρ Ἀλλὰ μὴν τό γε αἴτιον ὦ Ἱππία καὶ οὗ ἂν αἴτιον
greek_tokens: aitios eimi o kalos eimi gar alla meis o ge aitios eimi ippios kai e an aitios eimi o aitios allos eimi ou gar pou o ge aitios aitios aitios an eaw wde de skopaw ou o aitios poiew fainw panu ge oukoun upo o poiew poiew ou allos tis h o gignomai alla ou o poiew eimi outos oukoun allos tis o gignomai allos de o poiew nai ou ara o ge aitios aitios
english_head: Yes, it is. But surely, Hippias, the cause and that of which the cause is the cause are different; for the cause could not well be the cause of the cause. But look at it in this way was not the cause seen to be creating? Yes, certainly. By that which creates, then, only that is created which comes into being, but not that which creates. Is not that true? That is true. The cause, then, is not the cause of the cause, but of that which comes into being through it.

greek_head: αἰτίου ἐστίν ἀλλὰ τοῦ γιγνομένου ὑφ' ἑαυτοῦ Πάνυ γε Εἰ ἄρα τὸ καλόν ἐστιν αἴτιον ἀγαθοῦ γίγνοιτ' ἂν
greek_tokens: aitios eimi alla o gignomai ufh eautou panu ge ei ara o kalos eimi aitios agaqos gignomai an upo o kalew o agaqos kai dia outos ws eoika spoudazw kai o fronhsis kai allos pas o kalos ostis o ergnumi autos kai o ekgonos spoudastos eimi o agaqos kai kinduneuw ek os euriskw en pathr tis idea eimi o kalos o agaqos panu men oun kalos gar legw eimi swkraths oukoun kai ode kalos legos ostis oute o pathr us
english_head: Certainly. If, then, the beautiful is the cause of good, the good would come into being through the beautiful; and this is why we are eager for wisdom and all the other beautiful things, because their offspring, the good, is worthy of eagerness, and, from what we are finding, it looks as if the beautiful were a sort of father of the good. Certainly for what you say is well said, Socrates. Then is this well said, too, that the father is not the son, and the son not father?

greek_head: ἐστιν οὔτε ὁ ὑὸς πατήρ Καλῶς μέντοι Οὐδέ γε τὸ αἴτιον γιγνόμενόν ἐστιν οὐδὲ τὸ γιγνόμενον αὖ αἴτιον
greek_tokens: eimi oute o us pathr kalos mentoi oude ge o aitios gignomai eimi oude o gignomai au aitios alhqhs legw ma dios eimi aristos oude ara o kalos agaqos eimi oude o agaqos kalos h dokew su oios te eimi ek o proerew ou ma o dios ou egw fainw areskw oun egw kai eqelw an legw ws o kalos ou agaqos oude o agaqos kalos ou ma o dios ou panu egw areskw nai ma o dios eimi ippios egw de ge pas
english_head: To be sure it is well said. And neither is the cause that which comes into being, nor is that which comes into being the cause. True. By Zeus, my good friend, then neither is the beautiful good, nor the good beautiful; or does it seem to you possible, after what has been said? No, by Zeus, it does not appear so to me. Does it please us, and should we be willing to say that the beautiful is not good, and the good not beautiful? No, by Zeus, it does not please me at all. Right, by Zeus, Hippias!

greek_head: ἥκιστα ἀρέσκει ὧν εἰρήκαμεν λόγων Ἔοικε γὰρ οὕτως
greek_tokens: hkistos areskw os erw logos eoika gar outws kinduneuw ara egw ou wsper arti fainw kalos eimi o logos o wfelimos kai o xrhsimos te kai o dunatos agaqos tis poiew kalos eimi ou outws exw alla ei oios su eimi ekeinos eimi geloios o proteros en os o te parqenos oiomai eimi o kalos kai eis ekastos o emprosqen legw eoika kai egw men ge ou eti exw eimi ippios opoi trepw alla aforaw su de exis tis legw
english_head: And it pleases me least of all the things we have said. Yes, that is likely. Then there is a good chance that the statement that the beneficial and the useful and the powerful to create something good are beautiful, is not, as it appeared to be, the most beautiful of of statements, but, if that be possible, is even more ridiculous than those first ones in which we thought the maiden was the beautiful, and each of the various other things we spoke of before. That is likely. And Hippias, I no longer know where to turn; I am at a loss; but have you anything to say?

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔν γε τῷ παρόντι ἀλλ' ὥσπερ ἄρτι ἔλεγον σκεψάμενος εὖ οἶδ' ὅτι εὑρήσω
greek_tokens: ou en ge o pareimi alla wsper arti elegos skeptomai eu oida ostis euriskw alla egw egw dokazw upo epiqumia o oida ou oios te sos eimi perimenw mellw kai gar oun dh tis kai oimh arti euporew oraw gar ei o an xairw egw poiew mhtis passw o hdona alla o an dia o akoh kai o oyis outos fhmi eimi kalos pws tis ara an agwnizomai
english_head: Not at the moment, but, as I said just now, I am sure I shall find it after meditation. But it seems to me that I am so eager to know that I cannot wait for you while you delay; for I believe I have just now found a way out. Just see; how would it help us towards our goal if we were to say that that is beautiful which makes us feel joy; I do not mean all pleasures, but that which makes us feel joy through hearing and sight?

greek_head: οἵ τέ γέ που καλοὶ ἄνθρωποι ὦ Ἱππία καὶ τὰ ποικίλματα πάντα καὶ τὰ ζωγραφήματα καὶ τὰ πλάσματα
greek_tokens: e su ge pou kalos anqrwpos eimi ippios kai o poikilma pas kai o zwgrafhma kai o plasma terpos egw oraw o an kalos eimi kai e fqoggos e kalos kai o mousikh sumpas kai e logos kai o muqologia tautos outos ergazomai wste ei apokrinw o qraomai ekeinos anqrwpos ostis eimi gennaios o kalos eimi o dia akoh te kai dia oyis hdus ou an oiomai autos o qrasos episxw egw goun dokew nun ge eimi swkraths eu legw
english_head: For surely beautiful human beings, Hippias, and all decorations and paintings and works of sculpture which are beautiful, delight us when we see them; and beautiful sounds and music in general and speeches and stories do the same thing, so that if we were to reply to that impudent fellow, My excellent man, the beautiful is that which is pleasing through hearing and sight, don’t you think that we should put a stop to his impudence? To me, at any rate, Socrates, it seems

greek_head: τὸ καλὸν ὃ ἔστιν Τί δ' ἆρα τὰ ἐπιτηδεύματα τὰ καλὰ καὶ τοὺς νόμους
greek_tokens: o kalos o eimi tis de ara o epithdeuma o kalos kai o nomos eimi ippios dia akoh h dia oyis fhmi hdus eimi kalos eimi h allos tis eidos exw outos de isos eimi swkraths kan paralanqanw o anqrwpos ma o kuwn eimi ippios ou os ge an egw malista aisxunw lhrew kai prospoiew tis legw mhdeis legos tis outos o swfroniskos os egw oudeis an mallon outos
english_head: that the nature of the beautiful is now well stated. But what then? Shall we say, Hippias, that beautiful customs and laws are beautiful because they are pleasing through hearing and sight, or that they have some other form of beauty? Perhaps, Socrates, these things might slip past the man unnoticed. No, by dog, Hippias—not past the man before whom I should be most ashamed of talking nonsense

greek_head: ἐπιτρέποι ἀνερεύνητα ὄντα ῥᾳδίως λέγειν ἢ ὡς εἰδότα ἃ μὴ οἶδα
greek_tokens: epitrepw anereunhtos eimi radios legw h ws oida o mh oida alla meis egw kai autos epei su eipon dokew tis allos eimi outos o peri o nomos exis hsuxaw eimi ippios kinduneuw gar su en o autos empitnw aporia peri o kalew en hper nundi oiomai en allh tis euporia eimi pws outos legw eimi swkraths egw su frazw o ge egw katafainw ei ara tis
english_head: and pretending that I was talking sense when I was not. What man is that? Socrates, the son of Sophroniscus, who would no more permit me to say these things carelessly without investigation than to say that I know what I do not know. But certainly I also, now that you have mentioned it, think that this about the laws is something different. Not too fast, Hippias; for very likely we have fallen into the same perplexity about the beautiful in which we were a while ago, although we think we have found another way out. What do you mean by that, Socrates? I will tell you what presents itself to me, if perhaps there may be some sense in it.

greek_head: λέγω ταῦτα μὲν γὰρ τὰ περὶ τοὺς νόμους τε καὶ τὰ ἐπιτηδεύματα τάχ' ἂν φανείη οὐκ ἐκτὸς ὄντα τῆς αἰσθήσεως ἣ
greek_tokens: legos outos men gar o peri o nomos te kai o epithdeuma taxa an fainw ou ektos eimi o aisqhsis o dia o akoh te kai oyis egw eimi tugxanw alla upomenw outos o logos o dia outos hdus kalos eimi mhdeis o o nomos eis mesos paragw alla ei egw eromai eite outos os legos eite allos ostis tis dh eimi ippios te kai swkraths aforizw o hdus o outos hdus h legw kalos eimi o de kata o allos
english_head: For perhaps these matters of laws and customs might be shown to be not outside of the perception which we have through hearing and sight; but let us stick to the statement that that which is pleasing through the senses is beautiful, without interjecting the matter of the laws. But if this man of whom I speak, or anyone else whosoever, should ask us: Hippias and Socrates, did you make the distinction that in the category of the pleasing that which is pleasing in the way you mention is beautiful, whereas you say that that which is pleasing according to the other senses

greek_head: αἰσθήσεις σίτων τε καὶ ποτῶν καὶ τῶν περὶ τἀφροδίσια καὶ τἆλλα πάντα τὰ τοιαῦτα οὔ φατε καλὰ εἶναι ἢ οὐδὲ ἡδέα
greek_tokens: aisqhsis sitos te kai poths kai o peri afrodisios kai allos pas o toioutos ou fhmi kalos eimi h oude hdus oude hdona o parapan en o toioutos fatos eimi oude en allos h o eidon te kai akew tis fhmi eimi ippios pantws dhpou fhmi eimi swkraths kai en o allos megas panu hdona eimi tis oun fhmi hdona eimi oudeis hsswn h kai ekeinos afairew outos onoma kai aposterew o
english_head: —those concerned with food and drink and sexual love and all such things—is not beautiful? Or do you say that such things are not even pleasing and that there is no pleasure at all in them, nor in anything else except sight and hearing? What shall we say, Hippias? Certainly, by all means, Socrates, we shall say that there are very great pleasures in the other things also. Why, then, he will say, if they are pleasures no less than the others,

greek_head: καλὰς εἶναι Ὅτι φήσομεν καταγελῴη ἂν ἡμῶν οὐδεὶς ὅστις οὔ εἰ φαῖμεν μὴ ἡδὺ εἶναι φαγεῖν ἀλλὰ καλόν καὶ

english_head: do you take from them this designation and deprive them of being beautiful? english_head: and countless other cases which, you know, I said appeared before my mind? To which group do you assign the beautiful? Or have you the same view about it as I? For to me it seems great foolishness that we collectively are beautiful, but each of us is not so, or that each of us is so, but both are not, or anything else of that sort. Do you choose in this way, as I do, or in some other way? In this way, Socrates. You choose well, Hippias, that we may be free from the need of further search;

greek_head: πλείονος ζητήσεως εἰ γὰρ τούτων γ' ἐστὶ τὸ καλόν οὐκ ἂν ἔτι εἴη τὸ δι' ὄψεως καὶ ἀκοῆς ἡδὺ καλόν ἀμφότερα

english_head: for if the beautiful is in this group, that which is pleasing through sight and hearing would no longer be the beautiful. For the expression through sight and hearing makes both collectively beautiful, but not each individually; and this was impossible, as you and I agree. Yes, we agree. It is, then, impossible that the pleasant through sight and hearing be the beautiful, since in becoming beautiful it offers an impossibility. That is true. Then tell us again, he will say, from the beginning,

greek_head: τούτου διημάρτετε τί φατε εἶναι τοῦτο τὸ καλὸν τὸ ἐπ' ἀμφοτέραις ταῖς ἡδοναῖς δι' ὅτι ταύτας πρὸ τῶν ἄλλων

english_head: since you failed this time; what do you say that this ’beautiful,’ belonging to both the pleasures, is, on account of which you honored them before the rest and called them beautiful? It seems to me, Hippias, inevitable that we say that these are the most harmless and the best of pleasures, both of them collectively and each of them individually; or have you anything else to suggest, by which they excel the rest? Not at all; for really they are the best. This, then, he will say, you say is the beautiful, beneficial pleasure? It seems that we do, I shall say; and you? I also. Well, then, he will say, beneficial is that which creates the good, but that which creates and that which is created were just now seen to be different, and our argument has come round to the earlier argument, has it not? For neither could the good be beautiful nor the beautiful good,

greek_head: εἴη καλὸν οὔτε τὸ καλὸν ἀγαθόν εἴπερ ἄλλο αὐτῶν ἑκάτερόν ἐστι Παντός γε μᾶλλον φήσομεν ὦ Ἱππία ἂν σωφρονῶμεν
greek_tokens: eaw kalos oute o kalos agaqos eiper allos autos ekateros eimi pas ge mallon fhmi eimi ippios an swfronew ou gar pou qemis o orqos legw mh sugxwrew alla dh ge eimi swkraths tis oiomai outos eimi sunapas knhsma o eimi kai peritmhma o logos osper arti elegos kata braxus diairew alla ekeinos kai kalos kai polus acios oios su eimi eu kai kalos logos kaqisthmi en dikasthrion h en bouleuthrion h epi allh
english_head: if each of them is different from the other. Absolutely true, we shall say, if we are reasonable; for it is inadmissible to disagree with him who says what is right. But now, Socrates, what do you think all this amounts to? It is mere scrapings and shavings of discourse, as I said a while ago, divided into bits; but that other ability is beautiful and of great worth, the ability to produce a discourse well and beautifully in a court of law or a council-house or before any other public body before which the discourse may be delivered,

greek_head: τινὶ ἀρχῇ πρὸς ἣν ἂν ὁ λόγος ᾖ πείσαντα οἴχεσθαι φέροντα οὐ τὰ σμικρότατα ἀλλὰ τὰ μέγιστα τῶν ἄθλων σωτηρίαν

english_head: to convince the audience and to carry off, not the smallest, but the greatest of prizes, the salvation of oneself, one’s property, and one’s friends. For these things, therefore, one must strive, renouncing these petty arguments, that one may not, by busying oneself, as at present, with mere talk and nonsense, appear to be a fool. My dear Hippias, you are blessed because you know the things a man ought to practise, and have, as you say, practised them satisfactorily. But I, as it seems, am possessed by some accursed fortune,

greek_head: φῄς ἐμὲ δὲ δαιμονία τις τύχη ὡς ἔοικε κατέχει ὅστις πλανῶμαι μὲν καὶ ἀπορῶ ἀεί ἐπιδεικνὺς δὲ τὴν ἐμαυτοῦ

english_head: so that I am always wandering and perplexed, and, exhibiting my perplexity to you wise men, am in turn reviled by you in speech whenever I exhibit it. For you say of me, what you are now saying, that I busy myself with silly little matters of no account; but when in turn I am convinced by you and say what you say, that it is by far the best thing to be able to produce a discourse well and beautifully and gain one’s end in a court of law or in any other assemblage,

english_head: I am called everything that is bad by some other men here and especially by that man who is continually refuting me; for he is a very near relative of mine and lives in the same house. So whenever I go home to my own house, and he hears me saying these things, he asks me if I am not ashamed that I have the face to talk about beautiful practices, when it is so plainly shown, to my confusion, that I do not even know what the beautiful itself is. And yet how are you to know, he will say, either who produced a discourse,

greek_head: ὅστις καλῶς κατεστήσατο ἢ μή ἢ ἄλλην πρᾶξιν ἡντινοῦν τὸ καλὸν ἀγνοῶν καὶ ὁπότε οὕτω διάκεισαι οἴει σοι κρεῖττον

english_head: or anything else whatsoever, beautifully, or not, when you are ignorant of the beautiful? And when you are in such a condition, do you think it is better for you to be alive than dead? So it has come about, as I say, that I am abused and reviled by you and by him. But perhaps it is necessary to endure all this, for it is quite reasonable that I might be benefited by it. So I think, Hippias, that I have been benefited by conversation with both of you; for I think I know the meaning of the proverb beautiful things are difficult.
